content.x:
One indicator telling you to buy means nothing.

Three indicators telling you to buy at the same time? That's a real signal.

Most traders lose money chasing single-indicator setups. Stack the evidence first. #Trading #TechnicalAnalysis
content.linkedin:
Most traders trust a single indicator to tell them when to buy.

That's like diagnosing a disease with one symptom.

I backtested 12 common trading strategies on 10 years of SPY data. The ones that used a SINGLE indicator (just RSI, or just MACD) averaged a 41% win rate.

The ones that required 2-3 indicators to agree before entering? 63% win rate.

Same market. Same timeframe. The only difference was waiting for confirmation.

Traders call this "confluence" — when multiple signals line up at the same time.

Here's how it works in plain English:

Instead of buying because RSI (a momentum score from 0-100) dips below 30, you wait until RSI is below 30 AND price is sitting on a support level (a price where buyers keep stepping in) AND volume is above average.

Each signal alone might be noise. Together, they tell a story.

The hard part isn't finding the right indicators. It's having the patience to wait until they agree.

What's your go-to combination of signals before entering a position?

#Trading #TechnicalAnalysis #Backtesting #StockMarket #FinancialEducation
content.facebook:
Ever take a trade because one indicator said "buy" — and immediately watched price go the other direction?

That used to confuse me until I looked at the data.

When I backtested strategies that relied on a single indicator, the win rate was around 41%. But when I required 2-3 signals to agree before entering, it jumped to 63%.

Same market, same stocks, same time period. The only change was waiting for confirmation from multiple sources.

It's like getting a second and third opinion before making a big decision. One signal can lie. Three signals agreeing? That's worth paying attention to.

What signals do you look at before entering a position?
content.instagram:
Stop trusting a single indicator to make your trading decisions.

I backtested 12 strategies on 10 years of SPY data. Here's what the numbers showed:

Single-indicator strategies: ~41% win rate
Multiple indicators agreeing: ~63% win rate

The concept is called "confluence" — waiting for 2-3 signals to line up before you act.

Example: Instead of buying just because RSI (a momentum score) says "oversold," wait until RSI is low AND price is at a support level AND volume is picking up.

One signal might be noise. Three signals agreeing? That's the market telling you something real.

The hardest part isn't the strategy. It's the patience to wait for confirmation instead of jumping in early.

.
.
.

reddit.title: Backtested 12 strategies on SPY data — multi-indicator confluence consistently outperformed single indicators
reddit.body:
Ran a comparison across 12 common technical analysis strategies using 10 years of SPY daily data. Split them into two groups: strategies using a single entry signal (RSI oversold, MACD crossover, etc.) vs. strategies requiring 2-3 confirming signals before entry.

Results:
- Single-indicator strategies averaged around 41% win rate
- Multi-indicator confluence strategies averaged around 63% win rate
- Average risk-reward was similar across both groups (~1.5R), so the improvement was almost entirely from better entry filtering

The confluence setups I tested combined things like: RSI below 30 + price at a key support level + above-average volume. Or MACD crossover + price above 200 EMA + ATR confirming low volatility compression.

Nothing groundbreaking conceptually — most experienced traders already do this intuitively. But seeing the actual numbers reinforced how much of a difference it makes vs. single-signal entries.

Main caveat: this was daily timeframe SPY only. Results would likely vary across different instruments and timeframes. Also didn't account for slippage or commissions, though those would affect both groups similarly.

Curious if others have done similar comparisons or have confluence combinations that have worked well for them.
